coredata.dc:title: Surface state-regulated redox carbon nanodots for plasmonic morphology-dependent ratiometric sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yu Zhu
Liu, Shi Gang
Zhang, Ying
Ren, Wang
Sun, Zhe
Li, Bang Lin
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: Controlled-synthesis of carbon nanodots (CNDs) with tunable properties has always been challenging. Here, novel CNDs (blue-emitting CNDs (b-CNDs) and green-emitting CNDs (g-CNDs)) with tunable redox abilities and emission are synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method. The redox ability of CNDs is regulated by surface state tailoring. The abundant reductive groups on the surfaces endow the b-CNDs appealing reducibility, whereas g-CNDs with fewer surface functional groups are deficient in reducibility. Using HAuCl4 indicator, the distinct reducibility of CNDs is visually identified. Gold salts are reduced to Au nanobelts under reduction and stabilization of b-CNDs, causing plasmons-induced color changes, whereas g-CNDs have no reduction effect on Au3+. The size-evolution process of Au nano-species can guide not only plasmonic absorbance alteration but also fluorescence (FL) and second-order scattering (SOS) changes. Therefore, based on the plasmonic morphology-induced SOS increasement and the Förster resonance energy transfer-induced FL switch off, the b-CNDs contribute to a ratiometric sensor coupled with FL and SOS for Au3+ detection. The ratiometric strategy displays a better sensitivity and selectivity and a broader linear range than single-signal SOS or FL method. The regulation of redox CNDs and ratiometric assay of Au3+ provide a new prospect for the preparation and application of versatile CNDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Redox carbon nanodots
Reducibility visualization
Tunable fluorescence
Plasmonic nanocrystals
Fluorescence and scattering ratiometric sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of different emission and reducibility
Formation and mechanism of Au nanobelts produced with b-CNDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the funding provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis process of b-CNDs and g-CNDs.

The absorption (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of (A) b-CNDs and (C) g-CNDs. Insets: photographs taken under visible light (left) and under 365 nm UV light (right). Fluorescence emission spectra of (B) b-CNDs and (D) g-CNDs with different excitation wavelengths. (E) CIE coordinates of two typical CNDs. [b-CNDs] = 3.8% v/v; [g-CNDs] = 6% v/v.

TEM images and particle size distribution of (A) b-CNDs and (B) g-CNDs. Inset of B: HRTEM image of g-CNDs. (C) Raman spectra of the two typical CNDs. High-resolution XPS C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s spectra of (D) b-CNDs and (E) g-CNDs. (F) XPS data analyses of the two typical CNDs.

FL-SOS (A) and absorption (B) spectra and the corresponding photographs (insets in A and B) of b-CNDs before and after the incubation of Au3+ for 24 h. Conditions: [b-CNDs] = 3.8% v/v; [Au3+] = 458 μM; pH 7.0 (HCl/NaOH). (C) The TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of Au nanoparticles reduced by b-CNDs. (D) High-resolution XPS Au 4f spectra of Au nanoparticles.

(A) The photographs under visible light (left), TEM images (right) and, (B) diagram of the growth process of reduced Au nanocrystals by b-CNDs within 0.5 h, 10 h, and 24 h. Conditions: [b-CNDs] = 3.8% v/v, [Au3+] = 458 μM, pH = 7.0 (HCl/NaOH). (C) Scheme of the evolution procedure, (D) TEM images and photographs (under visible light) of Au nanocrystals reduced by b-CNDs within 5 min, 10 min, and 30 min in BR buffer (pH 7.0). (E) HRTEM image of Au aggregation with 30 min incubation. (F) The F/S of b-CNDs (3.8% v/v) in the presence of Au3+ (458 μM) in BR buffer (pH 7.0) for 30 min and in aqueous solution (HCl/NaOH, pH 7.0) for 90 min. (G) the FL and SOS spectra of BR buffer (pH 7.0), Au3+ (458 μM) in BR buffer, and b-CNDs (3.8% v/v)/Au3+ in BR buffer for 30 min reaction.
Schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching and SOS increasement of b-CNDs induced by Au3+.

(A) FL and SOS spectra of b-CNDs with the introduction of Au3+ with various concentrations from 0 to 680 μM. (B) Plot of F/S versus Au3+ concentration. Inset: linear relationship of the F/S vs. Au3+ concentration in the range of 0.2–230 μM. (C) Comparation of the analytical performances for Au3+ monitoring based on single FL or SOS response method as well as ratiometric strategy. (D) Selectivity of b-CNDs for Au3+ over species, the concentration of all analytes is 458 μM. 0–16 denote the cations of Cr3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Au3+, and Ag+, respectively. Conditions: [b-CNDs] = 3.8% v/v; [Au3+] = 458 μM; BR buffer, pH 7.0; λ ex = 360 nm; λ em = 440 nm.

head.ce:title: Surface state-regulated redox carbon nanodots for plasmonic morphology-dependent ratiometric sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha 410128, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Sichuan University of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Controlled-synthesis of carbon nanodots (CNDs) with tunable properties has always been challenging. Here, novel CNDs (blue-emitting CNDs (b-CNDs) and green-emitting CNDs (g-CNDs)) with tunable redox abilities and emission are synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method. The redox ability of CNDs is regulated by surface state tailoring. The abundant reductive groups on the surfaces endow the b-CNDs appealing reducibility, whereas g-CNDs with fewer surface functional groups are deficient in reducibility. Using HAuCl4 indicator, the distinct reducibility of CNDs is visually identified. Gold salts are reduced to Au nanobelts under reduction and stabilization of b-CNDs, causing plasmons-induced color changes, whereas g-CNDs have no reduction effect on Au3+. The size-evolution process of Au nano-species can guide not only plasmonic absorbance alteration but also fluorescence (FL) and second-order scattering (SOS) changes. Therefore, based on the plasmonic morphology-induced SOS increasement and the Förster resonance energy transfer-induced FL switch off, the b-CNDs contribute to a ratiometric sensor coupled with FL and SOS for Au3+ detection. The ratiometric strategy displays a better sensitivity and selectivity and a broader linear range than single-signal SOS or FL method. The regulation of redox CNDs and ratiometric assay of Au3+ provide a new prospect for the preparation and application of versatile CNDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), as a rising star of the carbon nanomaterial family, have attracted much interest on account of their admirable physical and chemical properties, including luminescence, biocompatibility, stability, and easy preparation [1]. Much efforts have been put into the multifarious applications and preparation techniques of CNDs [2–4]. However, two important and difficult challenges remain, namely, controlled-synthesis and luminescence mechanism. Recently, several scientists have reported meaningful researches concerning the above issues. Sun and colleagues obtained full-color carbon dots by tuning thermal-pyrolysis temperature and reactant ratio, and ascribed the red shift of fluorescence to controlled graphitization and surface carboxyl [5]. They also synthesized white fluorescent carbon dots, and explored that the photoluminescence mechanism was the formation of H- and J-aggregates and Förster resonance energy transfer between the CDs [6]. Zboril et al. prepared a series of carbon dots with multiple color emission using one-pot method followed by column chromatography to separate them, and assigned the red-shifted emission to the increasement of graphitic nitrogen amount [7]. These studies were only aimed at preparing carbon dots with multiple fluorescence. As far as we know, the controllable-synthesis of CNDs with adjustable redox ability has not been reported up to now.

Gold nanomaterials have gained persistent interests and wide applications in virtue of their unique properties [8]. Accordingly, the development of routs to synthesize multiform gold-based nanomaterials is another attractive subject for researchers. CNDs with affluent surface groups and nanoscale dimension can be employed as both the reducer and stabilizer for the preparation of Au nanomaterials without complicated procedures or additional energy, which is simpler than many reducing agents [9,10]. Moreover, the CNDs reduction method is conducive to the formation of metal–carbon radical nanohybrids which may endow the resulting Au nanostructure unordinary physicochemical property and improved biocompatibility [11,12]. However, majority of the reported CNDs are free of reducibility. Hence, the preparation and identification of reduced CNDs are still of great significance.

On the other hand, the extensive utilization produces gold pollution that cannot be neglected. Some gold residues which remain in obsoleting electronic devices, inactive catalyzers and other materials can leach to soil and water by some ways. Metallic gold usually was deemed to possess excellent stability. However, some studies have proved that metallic gold can be chemically oxidized to Au cations by strong oxidants [13]. It is worth stressing that ionic gold species, especially Au3+, show highly toxic to living organisms due to the strong binding between Au3+ and DNA/enzymes [14]. Therefore, the establishment of efficient methods for Au3+ sensing and simultaneous Au3+ reduction to mitigate toxicity is very desirable. In this regard, probing technique for Au3+ sensing has been developed [15,16]. However, majority of Au3+-response optical probes only use fluorescence as a single read-out signal, which may lead to unfaithful results interfered by background signal and the fluctuation of instrumental conditions. Ratiometric probes, possessing a built-in correction via simultaneously collecting two or more detection signals, can eliminate interference signals and therefore increases signal fidelity [17,18]. To date, only a few Au3+-sensitive ratiometric assays have been reported, mainly using small organic molecule fluorescent probes [19,20]. The complex synthesis and poor water solubility of organic small molecule-based probes go against their practical applications. In addition, traditional nanomaterial-based ratiometric assays are usually assembled with two or more responsive elements by physical or chemical method, which bear complicated preparation and considerable interactions among the compositive responsive elements [21,22]. Thus, it is significant to design a novel ratiometric strategy for Au3+ detection with good sensitivity, selectivity, and simple preparation.

Herein, redox ability-modulated CNDs with blue and green emission (b-CNDs and g-CNDs) were synthesized using one-pot hydrothermal method followed by separation with column chromatography. The distinct optical properties of blue- and green-emitting CNDs (b-CNDs, g-CNDs) are due to the effect of the graphitization degree. In addition, the redox abilities of CNDs are regulated by the surface state tailoring. The abundant reductive groups on the surfaces endow the b-CNDs appealing reducibility, whereas the g-CNDs with few surface functional groups are deficient in reducibility. With the aid of an indicator of HAuCl4, the adjustable reducibility of CNDs is visually identified. Gold salts are reduced to Au nanobelts under the reduction and stabilization of b-CNDs, resulting in the plasmons-induced color variations, whereas g-CNDs possess no effect on Au3+. The Au nanostructures generated in situ cause the increase of second-order scattering (SOS), meanwhile the electron transfer results in the fluorescence quenching of b-CNDs. Accordingly, we coupled SOS with FL to develop a ratiometric assay for Au3+ sensing. Unlike the conventional ratiometric sensor composed of multi-signal unites, the single component sensor (b-CNDs) can respond to the target with dual signals, eliminating the complex establishment process of ratiometric sensor. Heretofore, no literature about FL-SOS ratiometric method for Au3+ response is reported. Totally, this work provides a new perspective for versatile CND preparation and ratiometric sensing applications.

The CNDs with different emission and ruducibility were synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal treatment of norepinephrine (NE) and ethylenediamine (EDA) (see Supporting Information). Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation process of CNDs. NE contains a redox-active catechol group and a condensable ethanolamine moiety [1]. EDA, with abundant amino groups, is a frequently-used organic base. The addition of EDA not only increases the solubility of NE in ultrapure water, but also promotes the oxidation of catechol groups into quinone. Afterwards, they continue to copolymerize and carbonate to form CNDs under high temperature and high pressure. Followed by silica column chromatography, two kinds of CNDs with different fluorescence intensity and polarity were obtained. Compared to g-CNDs eluted with acetidine/methanol (volume ratio = 15:1), the b-CNDs were eluted with pure carbinol, indicating the higher polarity of b-CNDs. As shown in Fig. 1 A and 1C, the obtained b-CNDs and g-CNDs possess absorption in the UV–vis range of 260–300 nm, indicating the π-π* transitions of C = C bonds [23]. During the lower energy region of 300–400 nm, b-CNDs display broad and uncharacteristic absorption band compared with g-CNDs, reflecting more amorphous structures or complex surface states of b-CNDs than g-CNDs [24,25]. Correspondingly, their optimal fluorescence excitation positions are close to the absorption band in the lower energy region. Under the optimal excitation, the two typical CNDs exhibit blue and green fluorescence, respectively, and the chromaticity coordinates are (0.168, 0.174) and (0.248, 0.565) in the Commission Internationale de l’Éclairage (CIE) color space, respectively (Fig. 1E). The 3D fluorescence spectra further reveal their respective relationships of fluorescence excitation and emission (Fig. S1A and S1B). Specifically, the fluorescence emission of g-CNDs is excitation-independent, while that of b-CNDs is excitation-dependent (Fig. 1B and 1D). Affluent surface states or massive defect sites on CNDs provide various emission states, leading to excitation-dependent emission [26,27]. That is, g-CNDs and b-CNDs were given different types of core structure and surfer states during growth. On the other hand, the b-CNDs (7.23 ns) possess short and adjacent fluorescence lifetimes with g-CNDs (6.92 ns) (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of different emission and reducibility
ce:section.ce:para: The origin of different emission was explored from the morphology and size of CNDs by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) first. As depicted in Fig. 2 A and 2B, the two typical nanodots have good monodispersity and similar diameters. The result declared that accordant shapes and sizes are not the headstream of different fluorescence emissions. Accordingly, quantum size effects were eliminated. As displayed in the inset of Fig. 2B, g-CNDs exhibit distinct lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.14 nm, which is close to (0 0 4) facet of sp2 graphitic carbon [28]. Unlike the crystalline g-CNDs, no clear lattice fringes are observed in b-CNDs, indicating that the amorphous structures are dominant in the core regions of b-CNDs [29,30]. As exhibited in Fig. 2C, the characteristic D and G bands are observed in Raman spectra, which denote disordered structure and graphitic structure in carbon-based materials, respectively. The intensity ratio of G band to D band (I G/I D) increases from 1.79 (b-CNDs) to 2.60 (g-CNDs), indicating that the graphitization degree of g-CNDs is significantly higher than that of b-CNDs, which may be closely related to the emission of red-shift fluorescence [6]. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) show that the b-CNDs and g-CNDs consisted of the same elements of C (284 eV), N (399 eV), and O (531 eV) atoms (Fig. S2). In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2D1 and 2E1), the C 1 s band includes 3 peaks at 285, 286, and 288 eV, representing C–C/C = C, C–N/C–O, and C = O, respectively [2]. The N 1 s spectrum can be deconvoluted into C–N = C (399 eV), C–N–C (401 eV), and amino N (402 eV), respectively (Fig. 2D2 and 2E2). For O 1 s, the XPS spectrum can be fitted into 2 oxygen species of C = O (531 eV) and C-O (533 eV) (Fig. 2D3 and 2E3) [3]. As depicted in Fig. 2F, the carbon element content of g-CNDs is 11.83% higher than that of b-CNDs, and the C = C/C–C content is even twice as high as that of b-CNDs. These results reveal the higher carbonation and graphitization of g-CNDs. High carbonation extent suggests the massive release of oxygen and nitrogen, then boosting graphitization during growth. In the light of the above Raman and XPS characterizations, the red shift of FL follows the increase of carbonation and graphitization from b-CNDs to g-CNDs, confirming that the degree of carbonation and graphitization is closely related to the emission location of CNDs. Higher graphitization extent can broaden sp2 domain and extend effective conjugation length, producing longer wavelength emission [6,31].

It is noteworthy that surface states lay a key foundation for the functions of CNDs, such as redox ability. Firstly, the surface groups of CNDs were identified with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurement. As depicted in Fig. S3, both blue- and green-emitting CNDs possess O–H (3410 cm−1), N–H (3210 cm−1), C = C/C = N (1636–1598 cm−1), and C = N–O (1420 cm−1), which indicate the formation of conjugated structure with O- and N-containing groups in the CNDs [32,33]. Concretely, the wide absorption band of b-CNDs in the range 3410–3210 cm−1 is attributed to the overlap of strong absorptions of O − H and N − H bonds. However, an extremely weak peak is observed at 3210 cm−1 in g-CNDs. It demonstrates that amino and hydroxyl groups with higher content exist in b-CNDs. Moreover, the abundant –OH and –NH2 cause higher polarity of b-CNDs, which is consistent with the column chromatography results. In addition, the N1s column in Fig. 2F reveals that nitrogen atoms into g-CNDs are mainly doped in the form of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N, whereas b-CNDs possess more C-NH2 than g-CNDs in evidence. We suspect that the reductive N/O-containing groups, such as –NH2 and –OH, increase significantly from g-CNDs to b-CNDs, endowing the two CNDs with different reducibility. As a proof of concept, the different reducibility of b-CNDs and g-CNDs was confirmed with the Au3+ reduction (Fig. S4). Purple solution together with plasma absorption is observed with adding b-CNDs, suggesting the formation of Au nanoparticles. But no change was observed with the introduction of Au3+ in g-CNDs solution under the same conditions. Herein, the reducibility of CNDs can be visually discriminated with HAuCl4 indicator. The regulation of surface states endows the CNDs different reducibility between b-CNDs and g-CNDs. The poor surface states on g-CNDs render the lack of redox ability. In contrast, the b-CNDs possess abundant functional groups at the edge and defects, rationalizing its especial reducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and mechanism of Au nanobelts produced with b-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The reducibility of b-CNDs renders them a promising reducer for Au3+. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the b-CNDs emit strong fluorescence at 440 nm under 360 nm excitation. With the introduction of Au3+, the b-CNDs fluorescence quenches, and more interestingly, the SOS centered at about 720 nm increases concomitantly. As is known, the generation/disintegration of bigger-size structures can alter the scattering properties [34]. We therefore infer that Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) may be formed. As depicted in Fig. 3B (inset), after the addition of Au3+, the significant color transfer of b-CNDs solution from pale yellow to gray purple is observed. Moreover, the emerging absorption band at 530 nm is assigned to characteristic plasma absorption of Au NPs. The broad band width suggests the asymmetrical shapes and sizes of formed Au NPs. To identify the generated Au particles through microscopic technique, TEM characterization was then conducted. As directly illustrated in Fig. 3C, Au nanoparticles with irregular shapes and a major dimensional range of 10–30 nm are formed. HRTEM image reveals the typical lattice space of 0.24 nm which is corresponding to the (1 1 1) lattice space of metallic Au (JCPDS 04–0784) [35]. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS of Au 4f shows typical Au 4f7/2 (83.7 eV) and Au 4f5/2 (87.3 eV) peaks, indicating the metallic state of the formed gold nanoparticles (Fig. 3D) [36].

The growth process of Au nanoparticles reduced by b-CNDs was then surveyed. HAuCl4 disassociated in water as H+ and AuCl4 –. The zeta potential of b-CNDs was found to be 10.90 mV. Thus, the positively charged b-CNDs and the negatively charged AuCl4 – are drawn closer by electrostatic attraction, promoting their further redox reactions. When reaction time ranges from 0.5 h to 24 h, the solution color varies from buff, light orange to gray purple (left column of Fig. 4 A), and the plasma absorption strengthens progressively (Fig. S5). TEM images identify the proceeding morphological changes (right column of Fig. 4A). Ultra-small Au nanodots (0.5 h) are observed firstly, then nano particles with average diameter of 10 nm are surveyed within 10 h reaction. After 24 h, irregular Au nanobelts emerge as the dominant appearances. As shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. S6), the necklace-liked Au nanobelts are strung with Au nanospheres. Accordingly, we inferred that Au nanocrystal growth goes through the following stages. Under the action of reduction and stabilization of b-CNDs, Au crystal nucleuses were created first, and then grew into quasi-spherule nanoparticles. Subsequently, the formed nano-spherules develop into longer Au nanobelts via attachment and fusion (Fig. 4B) [37]. The primary driving force of the attachment-based crystal growth emanates from surface energy reduction [38]. Neutral pH value can eliminate efficaciously electrostatic repulsion and modulate surface energy between the adjacent Au NPs, consequently boosting the attachment-based Au nanobelts growth. Herein, pH 7.0 is selected in the whole system which is regulated with NaOH (0.02 M) and HCl (0.02 M). Under the same conditions, no typical absorption, fluorescence quenching, and SOS enhancement are observed without b-CNDs (Fig. S7). The control experiments suggested that b-CNDs play indispensable role in Au nanobelts formation.

When the BR buffer (pH 7.0, 0.04 M) was selected to regulate pH instead of NaOH/HCl, the color of the solution surprisingly changes from pale yellow to gray within only 30 min (right column of Fig. 4D). The absorption with broad range of 400–700 increases gradually, consistent with the change in color of the solution (Fig. S8). TEM images reveal that the onsite-reduced Au nanodots become into irregular large congeries via the concurrence of attachment-based growth and aggregation (Fig. 4D and 4E). The aggregation behavior then induces remarkable scattering enhancement (Fig. 4G). However, no SOS increases is observed without b-CNDs, revealing that BR buffer is an important accelerator of Au growth and aggregation, but hold no liability for the Au reduction. As is known, phosphate, borate, and acetate anions with lone-pair electron have eminent metal-binding properties. The binding of these anions with Au modulates electrostatic interaction and attachment surface, consequently accelerating the growth and aggregation of Au NPs. Fig. 4C exhibits the evolution process of Au nanostructures within BR buffer.

The FL-SOS dual-signal reversed change of b-CNDs provides the potentiality to develop a ratiometric sensing platform for Au3+ monitoring. As shown in Fig. 4F, after adding BR buffer for 30 min, the ratio of SOS to FL intensity (S/F) is more than four times that with NaOH/HCl regulation for 90 min incubation, indicating more superior sensitivity and faster response rate for Au3+ detection.

The origination of FL-SOS dual-signal response was investigated. As shown in Fig. S9A, in the absence and presence of Au3+, the lifetime of b-CNDs is observed to decrease from 7.23 to 6.04 ns. Accordingly, the FL of the b-CNDs was switch off by the dynamic quenching. Generally, when the emission spectrum of a donor overlaps with the absorption spectrum of an acceptor and the donor–acceptor pair is at a specific distance, Förster resonance energy transfer will happen from the donor to the acceptor [6]. Fig. S9B displays that the FL emission spectrum of b-CNDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of Au nanostructures. During the generation of Au nanostructures, the b-CNDs serve as both the reducer and stabilizer, which renders b-CNDs adjoin closely Au nanostructures, consequently making the Förster resonance energy transfer possible. Hence, we infer that the FL quenching originates from the resonance energy transfer. For scattering, it is closely relative to the particle volume and shape in the light of Rayleigh scattering theory, which is expressed as following equation (1) [39]:
(1)
I =
24
π
3
ν
2
N
λ
4
n
1
2
-
n
0
2
n
1
2
+ 2
n
0
2
2
I 0
where I and I 0 are the intensities of scattering light and excitation light, respectively. ν denotes the volume of nanoparticles. N is the number of nanoparticles per unit volume. λ is wavelength. n 1 and n 0 are refractive index of the scatterer and medium, respectively. According to the above formula, the scattering intensity is in proportion to the square of the particle volume, also is associated with the relative refractive index of the scatterer. Therefore, the generation and aggregation of Au nanostructures causes the remarkable enhancement of SOS intensity. Fig. 5 shows the mechanism of dual-signal response of Au3+.

Aiming at optimal sensing efficiency, some assay conditions were firstly optimized. As exhibited in Fig. S10, 30 min and pH 7.0 were selected as the appropriate incubation time and pH condition, respectively. Next, the sensitivity of ratiometric sensing was explored under optimum conditions. Fig. 6 A indicates that the FL declines and SOS ascends consecutively with the increase of Au3+ concentration. Fig. 6B shows S/F as a function of the introduced concentration of Au3+, [Au3+]. A good linear relationship between S/F and [Au3+] was found in the range of 0.2–230 μM (R2 = 0.9952) with a detection limit of 67 nM. To assess the superiority of ratiometric assay with single-signal sensing, the calibration curves of FL or SOS detection of Au3+ were obtained, which are exhibited in Fig. S11. And the contrast results are depicted in Fig. 6C. Compared to single-signal sensing, ratiometric assay displays exceptional advantages including wider linear range and higher sensitivity. In addition, the selectivity of the ratiometric probe towards Au3+ was explored in the presence of possible inorganic cations and anions. As displayed in Fig. 6D, the proposed ratiometric strategy possesses good anti-interference ability. Overall, by virtue of FL-SOS combined technique, the ratiometric assay is successfully established for selective and sensitive Au3+ monitoring based on reductive b-CNDs. Unlike the conventional ratiometric sensor composed of multi-signal unites, the single component sensor (b-CNDs) can respond to the target with dual signals, eliminating the complex establishment process of ratiometric sensor. Moreover, a comparison with the reported ratiometric Au3+ sensors is summarized in Table S2. It can be seen that the reported ratiometric sensors are all organic small molecules. Compared with them, the proposed sensor has merits in both preparation and detection, including one-step synthesis in aqueous solution, lower detection limit, wider linear range, and detection in aqueous media without any organic cosolvent.

The practicality of the proposed method was studied through applying to the Au3+ detection in real water sample, which was got from the Beibei reservoir of Chongqing. Since no Au3+ was detected in the water sample, standard recovery experiments were then conducted. Table S3 shows that the recoveries of Au3+ are in the range of 101–105%. The relative standard deviations (RSD) of 3 times repeated experiments are below 3%. The good reliability and repeatability of b-CNDs-based ratiometric strategy shows a prospective potential for sensitive Au3+ detection in real samples.

In summary, CNDs with tunable reducibility and emission have been synthesized with one-pot hydrothermal method. The red shift of emission was ascribed to the expansion of graphited core (sp2 domain) from b-CNDs to g-CNDs. And the discrepancy of reducibility was related to the tailoring of reductive surface groups. The abundant reductive groups on the surfaces endow the b-CNDs appealing reducibility, whereas the g-CNDs with few surface functional groups are deficient in reducibility. HAuCl4 as a simple indicator, visually identified the different reducibility of CNDs. Gold salts were reduced to Au nanobelts under the reduction and stabilization of b-CNDs, resulting in the plasmons-induced color variations, whereas g-CNDs possessed no effect on Au3+. The size-evolution process guided not only plasmonic absorbance but also fluorescence and SOS changes. Therefore, the b-CNDs contributed to a ratiometric sensor for heavy metal ion Au3+ detection based on plasmonic morphology-dependent SOS increasement and fluorescence switch off. The proposed ratiometric sensor has higher accuracy and simpler construction than the traditional ratiometric sensors, meanwhile possesses better sensitivity and wider linear range than single-signal assays. The regulation of redox carbon nanodots and the ratiometric assay of Au3+ provide a new prospect for the preparation and application of versatile CNDs.

Yu Zhu Fan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shi Gang Liu: Investigation, Validation. Ying Zhang: Resources, Investigation. Wang Ren: Visualization, Formal analysis. Zhe Sun: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Bang Lin Li: Formal analysis. Hong Qun Luo: Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Nian Bing Li: Supervision, Project administration, Data curation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.146715.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
